description: If the implication of A from A-implies-B implies A, then A must be true.

hints.text: This is **Peirce's Law**: $((A \to B) \to A) \to A$. It's a classical tautology — not provable in intuitionistic logic. The key is to assume $\lnot A$, derive a [contradiction|contradiction], and then use `dne`. Start by assuming the antecedent: $(A \to B) \to A$.
Assume the hypothesis: if $A \to B$ holds, then $A$ holds.
To reach $A$ via [double-negation|double negation], assume $\lnot A$ and derive a [contradiction|contradiction]. Start: assume $\lnot A$.
From $A \to \bot$ you can derive $A \to B$: assume $A$, get $\bot$ via [modus-ponens|modus ponens], then explode to $B$. First, assume $A$.
Apply $A \to \bot$ (step 3) to your fresh $A$ to reach $\bot$.
From $\bot$, derive any [formula|formula] — in particular, $B$ — using `ex-falso`.
[assumption|Discharge] $A$: you've proved that from $A$ follows $B$, i.e. $A \to B$.
Now use the big hypothesis from step 1: $(A \to B) \to A$. Apply [modus-ponens|modus ponens] with your freshly-derived $A \to B$ (step 7) to get $A$.
You now have $A$ (step 8) and $\lnot A$ (step 2), both with $\lnot A$ in [context|context] — the [contradiction|contradiction]. Apply `not-intro (not A)` to get $\lnot \lnot A$.
Strip the [double-negation|double negation] with `dne` to reveal $A$.
[assumption|Discharge] the big hypothesis to finish Peirce's Law.
